title: A constitution before full independence
content: In 1814, Norwegian elites drafted a constitution at Eidsvoll. Norway did not become fully independent then, but it gained a constitutional foundation that shaped later sovereignty.

title: Union with Sweden, but growing self-rule
content: After 1814, Norway entered a union with Sweden. Over the 1800s, Norwegian institutions, parliament, and national identity strengthened, creating pressure for full independence.

title: Peaceful dissolution of the union
content: In 1905, the union with Sweden was dissolved peacefully. Norway became fully sovereign and chose a constitutional monarchy with a parliamentary system.
next: Independence did not end security risks.

title: Occupation in World War II
content: Nazi Germany occupied Norway from 1940 to 1945. The occupation was a major national trauma, shaping postwar security policy and state legitimacy.

title: Reconstruction and welfare-state expansion
content: After 1945, Norway invested in institutions, infrastructure, and social systems. Broad political consensus helped build a stable welfare model and high social trust.
next: Then came a structural economic shift.

title: North Sea oil changed state capacity
content: Offshore petroleum discoveries transformed public finances and economic planning. Norway gradually built legal and fiscal mechanisms to manage resource income over generations.

title: Twice outside the EU, still integrated
content: Norway voted against joining the EU in two referendums. Yet through EEA arrangements and trade links, it stayed deeply integrated with European markets and regulation.
next: Today's history question is about transition.

title: From oil state to transition state
content: Modern Norwegian history is now about balancing petroleum legacy, climate commitments, Arctic strategy, and long-term fiscal sustainability.
next: History explains why Norway's institutions look the way they do.
